name: Flat lintel
description: A flat horizontal beam — a single course of stripped log, smooth stone, or stone slab — laid directly above a door or window opening to span the gap and transfer the wall load above onto the jambs at either side. The lintel is the simplest possible spanning element: no arch, no relieving triangle, no keystone, just a straight bearing member. It is the dominant opening detail in vernacular cottage construction (oak or spruce log lintels over plank doors), in rustic timber-frame work, and in modern flat-headed openings (smooth-stone or concrete-equivalent beams above aluminium-look frames). Spans are short — up to five blocks — because a flat beam in bending is weaker than an arch in compression. The lintel may sit flush with the wall or project one block beyond the jambs as a shallow shelf, reading as a small canopy.
